coredata.dc:title: Be-original break new ground: Fluorescence sensing of humic acid in natural water and soil by pitaya seed carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mao, Chunling
Zhao, Hanqing
Ye, Heng
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: Pitaya seed carbon dots (P-CDs) with good biocompatibility were synthesized by hydrothermal method using natural biological matrix as carbon source, which applied to the detection of humic acid (HA) for the first time. So far, there is no report on the use of biomatrix-derived carbon dots for the detection of HA. This method can bring less pollution to the detection process of HA, which is very important to ensure lower cost, environmental safety and minimized energy consumption. In addition, it was worth noting that, based on the concept of green chemistry, the preparation process of P-CDs is simple, and the fluorescence method is used for analysis. Based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism, the detection of HA in water and soil can be realized in a short time. The results matched those of high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), suggesting that it shows great potential in water and soil quality monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pitaya seeds carbon dots
Humic substances
Humic acid
Green chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenched of P-CDs by HA
Study the quenching mechanism of P-CDs by HA
Specificity, reproducibility and stability of P-CDs
Fluorescence detection of HA in water

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020-181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image of the P-CDs. Inset showed the lattice spacing of P-CDs. (b) The partical diameter distribution of the P-CDs. (c) The FT-IR spectra of P-CDs.
(a) The XPS characteristics of P-CDs. (b) The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, and (d) O 1 s.

(a) The UV–vis spectrum and the fluorescence excitation and emission wavelength spectrum of P-CDs and HA. (b) The fluorescence intensity of P-CDs in different concentration of NaCl. (c) photobleaching experiment of P-CDs. (d) The excitation dependent emission characteristics of P-CDs.

(a) The cells viability of MC, HCT-116, Hepa1-6 macrophages incubated with the concentrations of P-CDs (0、12.5、25、50 、100 µg/mL) for 24 h. (b) The microscope image of HCT-116 cells without P-CDs. (c) The microscope image of MC cells with 100 µg/mL P-CDs. (d) The microscope image of HCT-116 cells without P-CDs. (e) The microscope image of HCT-116 cells with 100 µg/mL P-CDs. (f) The microscope image of Hepa1-6 cells without P-CDs. (g) The microscope image of Hepa1-6 cells with 100 µg/mL P-CDs.
(a) The fluorescence intensity of P-CDs and HA at different incubation temperatures. (b) The fluorescence lifetime of P-CDs with HA (red curve) and without HA (black curve).

(a) In the presence of some potential interfering substances, the selectivity (yellow bar) and interference (green bar) value. (b) Continuously scanned emission spectra of the same P-CDs at an excitation wavelength of 374 nm; the inset was the corresponding peak magnification.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of P-CDs at the presence of different concentrations of HA. The inset showed the photos of P-CDs under UV light in the presence of different concentrations of HA. (b) The linear regression equation of P-CDs with HA. The inset showed that P-CDs can be used for semi-quantitative detection of HA. Plot of the relationship between F/F0 against HA concentration.
Illustration of the process and mechanism of determining HA.

head.ce:title: Be-original break new ground: Fluorescence sensing of humic acid in natural water and soil by pitaya seed carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning 110016, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning 110016, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scheme 1. Illustration of the process and mechanism of determining HA.

Pitaya seed carbon dots (P-CDs) with good biocompatibility were synthesized by hydrothermal method using natural biological matrix as carbon source, which applied to the detection of humic acid (HA) for the first time. So far, there is no report on the use of biomatrix-derived carbon dots for the detection of HA. This method can bring less pollution to the detection process of HA, which is very important to ensure lower cost, environmental safety and minimized energy consumption. In addition, it was worth noting that, based on the concept of green chemistry, the preparation process of P-CDs is simple, and the fluorescence method is used for analysis. Based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism, the detection of HA in water and soil can be realized in a short time. The results matched those of high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), suggesting that it shows great potential in water and soil quality monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Humic substances (HS) are natural macromolecular organic compounds existing in all kinds of water bodies and soil, which are the main representative component of dissolved organic matter (DOM). As the HS’s main structure, the carbon-rich organic structure of humic acid (HA) is highly functional [1].HA is an aromatic and aliphatic compound that binds the –COOH, –OH phenolate, –OH alcoholic groups, and allows quinone groups [2]. The lack of HA in soil will affect the decline of land productivity, which in turn affects yield and quality [3]. HA plays an important role in maintaining soil sustainability. However, HA, fulvic acid (FA), and other HA components can form HS, which can produce toxins and affects electromagnetic radiation [4]. Thus, it is harmful to aquatic organisms. In addition, HS can react with disinfectants to produce strong carcinogens of chloroform (THMs) in the process of disinfection of tap water[5], which is also one of the main environmental factors leading to Kashin Beck disease. Therefore, the establishment of high sensitivity and stability of the HA quantitative method is of great significance for the quality of soil and natural water.

At present, on-line solid-phase extraction method [6], electrochemical analysis [7], UV–vis absorption spectroscopy [8] and chromatography [9] have been developed for HA quantification. However, the on-line solid-phase extraction method needs special instruments and a complicated operation process. The electrochemical analysis process will be interfered by Fe3+ and Ca2+, thus affecting the accuracy of the analysis results. Due to the special structure of HA, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy has no selectivity for the analysis of HA and other aromatic compounds. The chromatographic analysis results are good, but the cost is very high, and the sample pretreatment process is complex. In contrast, the fluorescence sensor with simple pretreatment and rapid analysis speed shows outstanding performance for the detection of HA in natural water samples.

A class of zero-dimensional nanomaterials as carbon dots (CDs), are endowed with stable photoluminescence properties [10]. The core of CDs is composed of sp3 hybrid amorphous carbon and sp2 hybrid crystalline graphite. Moreover, the surface of CDs is filled with oxygen and nitrogen functional groups [11–13]. CDs have emerged as a significant research area since the discovery by Xu and co-workers in 2004 [14]. The light emission of CDs originates from the insertion of the sp2 hybrid domain in the carbon nucleus or the oxidation of surface groups [15]. The varying band-gap and semiconducting properties of CDs extend the applications in optoelectronics, photovoltaic devices, sensors, drug delivery, and bio-imagining [16–18].

The reaction conditions of CDs synthesis are mild, and the operation steps are convenient. Sustainable natural biological substrates can be used to prepare CDs including mushroom [19], orange peel [20], apple juice [21] as green carbon sources instead of chemical carbon sources, which have the characteristics of environmental protection, low cost, simple operation and so on, practicing the concept of green chemistry. At present, the research of pitaya mainly focuses on the cultivation of fruit trees, fresh fruit storage, juice and wine processing. The surplus pitaya seeds processed by factories are usually directly treated as waste [22], which results in great waste of resources. Pitaya seeds are rich in cellulose and carbohydrates, which can theoretically synthesize pitaya seeds CDs (P-CDs). In line with the development trend of green chemistry, it has broad prospects to prioritize the development of precursors of biomass energy degradation waste CDs.

Therefore, this paper aimed to establish a green and sensitive method for the quantitative detection of HA in water and soil, which can indirectly reflect the content of HS in water and soil. (Scheme. 1 ) The preparation technology of P-CDs was optimized. Meanwhile, the stability of P-CDs under different pH and ion intensity conditions was investigated. Finally, the detection range and detection limit were obtained. The method was successfully dedicated to quantitative detection of HA in water and soil. At the same time, the mechanism of HA-induced fluorescence quenching of P-CDs was further discussed.

Sodium chloride (NaCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were gained by Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was obtained from Rionlon (Tianjin) Chemical Co., ltd. (Tianjin, China). Humic acid (HA), fulvic acid (FA), amino acid (Ala, Leu, Val, Ser, Glu), paraoxon, paraquat, chlorfenapyr, fipronil were acquired from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acetic acid and acetonitrile were obtained from Tianjin Concord Reagent Co., ltd. (Tianjin, China). Pitaya was purchased from Shenyang city, Liaoning Province, China.

Ultraviolet–Visible absorption spectroscopy was acquired by a TU-1810 spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., ltd. China). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy was executed by Thermo Fisher Nicolet 6700 FT-IR (Thermo Fisher Co., Waltham, USA). The excitation as well as emission spectra were measured by F97 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai lengguang Technology Co., ltd., China). Results were compared using an Agilent-1200 high performance liquid chromatograph (Agilent Technologies, USA). Ultrasound was performed on KQ5200 ultrasonic cleaner (Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., ltd.). Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) images were taken by JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectra were recorded on a Thermo Fisher EscaLab 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Co., Waltham, USA).The cytotoxicity of P-CDs was noted by the Mode Varioskan LUX microplate reader (Singapore). The image of the P-CDs-labeled cells comes from EVOS-FL-Auto-2 inverted microscope (Invitrogen, USA) under ambient conditions.

P-CDs were synthesized by the ultrasound-assisted hydrothermal method. Firstly, 0.75 g dried pitaya seed powder was dispersed in 10 mL purified water and diverted into the 20 mL poly (tetra-fluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave, which was heated at 180 °C for 5 h. When the response was completed, the obtained product was cooled to room temperature. After centrifugation at 4200 rpm for 10 min, transferred it to a 50 mL centrifuge tube. The supernatant after centrifugation was filtered through a microporous membrane (0.22 μm) to obtain a brown solution. The filtrate was encouraged to be purified by the dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da) for 24 h and the water was changed every 4 h that needs to be replaced 6 times. Finally, the stock solution of P-CDs was obtained for further characterization and application.

Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) provides a simpler method than the MTT method for cell number detection and cytotoxicity test. A highly water-soluble tetrazolium salt WST-8 [2 - (2-methoxy-4-nitrobenzene) − 5 - (2,4-silylsulfophenyl) − 2H tetrazolium salt, monosodium salt] is reduced in the presence of an electronic medium to form a water-soluble orange mezzanine dye [23–25]. The absorbance of the dye at 450 nm is in proportion to the number of living cells in the medium. In order to evaluate the toxicity level of newly synthesized P-CDs, MC (smooth muscle cells), HCT-116 (human colon cancer cells) and Hepal-6 (mouse liver cancer cells) was selected as model cells. 5000–10000 cells were inoculated in each well on 96-well plate, and cultured for 24 h. Then, 100 μL of different concentrations of P-CDs solution (0、12.5、25、50、100 µg/mL) were added to each hole. Three parallel samples in each group were incubated for 24 h. Then, another 10 µL of CCK-8 solution was added to each hole, which then was put into an incubator. After incubating for 4 h at room temperature, gently sucked out the culture medium from each well, and shook it on a constant temperature shaker at 85 rpm for 15 min to fully dissolved in the water. Finally, the absorbance of each well at 450 nm was measured through a microplate reader, and the cell viability can be calculated according to formula: cell viability (%) = [(As-Ab) / (Ac-Ab)] × 100 (As = absorbance of tested sample, Ab = absorbance of blank sample, Ac = absorbance of control group sample). The morphological images of three kinds of cells before and after adding P-CDs were obtained by inverted microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenched of P-CDs by HA
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, a series of HA solutions with concentration gradient (0.0, 3.0, 5.0, 8.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, 50.0, 60.0, 70.0, 80.0, 90.0, 100.0, 120.0 mg/L) were prepared. Then, the P-CDs solution (0.5 mg/mL) was mixed with 1 mL HA solution, and the included solution was diluted to 3 mL. After vortexing for 8 min, under the environmental conditions, the fluorescence spectrum of the included solution was recorded. Due to the low content of HA in natural water, 10.0, 50.0, and 80.0 mg/L HA were added to the natural water samples to explore the recovery rate. HA existed relatively more in the soil, thus, the soil from campus flower bed and medicinal botanical garden were selected to detect HA. The outcomes were compared to those obtained using high performance liquid chromatography method (HPLC) method.

HPLC method: HA was chromatographically analyzed at 40℃ using a ZORBAX SB-C18 (250 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 μm) column.The diode array detector’s detecting wavelength was set to 292 nm.The mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile (A) and water with 0.1 % acetic acid (B; VA: VB = 90: 10), and the flow rate was 1.0 mL/min.For analysis, the final solution was filtered using membrane filters (0.22 μm).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) exhibited the morphology and the size of P-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a and Fig. 1b, the obtained P-CDs were highly monodisperse nanoparticles with lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, which corresponded to the graphitic (100) planes [26]. Moreover, the average particle diameter was 6.41 ± 0.83 nm (N = 24). The surface functional groups and structure of P-CDs were described by FT-IR (Fig. 1c). The absorption band at 3422 cm−1 was strong and blunt, which was ascribed to the OH stretching vibration [27]. The characteristic peak at 1663 cm−1 was related to the tensile vibration of CO [28]. The characteristic peak at 1400 cm−1 came from the CN tensile vibration of amide. The peak at 1105 cm−1 was attributed to the tensile vibration of COC. There was no significant characteristic peak of carboxyl group found in FI-IR spectra and speculated that the carboxyl groups were decarboxylated at high temperature.

The composition of the main elements of P-CDs and their chemical environment were further analyzed by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the full spectrum of XPS showed three characteristic peaks at 531.4, 399.6 and 284.5 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s and C1s, distinguishly. The results showed that P-CDs contained 21.15 % oxygen, 10.82 % nitrogen and 68.03 % carbon. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2b) could be fitted to three peaks value at 284.5 eV, 285.5 eV [29] and 288.1 eV [18], due to CO / CN, CO / CN and CC / CC bond energies [30]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) could be resolved to two fitting peaks at 399.4 eV and 400.9 eV, according to the bond of pyrrole-N and graphite-N, respectively [31]. The spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2d) showed two fitting peaks at 531.4 eV and 532.8 eV, which were caused by CO and C-OH bonds, partly [32]. The results showed that P-CDs were mixed with nitrogen and oxygen, and were rich in oxygen. The results of XPS spectrum analysis showed that P-CDs surface contained abundant oxygen and nitrogen active groups.

The optical properties of fluorescent nanomaterials are important factors affecting their applications in biological imaging and biomarkers. In UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 3 a), the shoulder peak at 267 nm was attributed to n-π* electron transition demonstrating the presence of carbonyl. The absorbance of P-CDs solution with HA was lower than that of P-CDs solution without HA, which may be due to the covalent interaction between carboxyl group of P-CDs and some functional amino, mercaptan or hydroxyl groups of HA [33]. The reason for the decrease in absorbance value may be through the interaction of intermolecular hydrogen bonds or covalent bonds (such as ester bonds or amide bonds). A bright blue fluorescent P-CDs solution can be seen under 365 nm ultraviolet light, and the solution was yellowish brown under environmental conditions. The maximum excitation wavelength was at 374 nm, as well as the maximum emission wavelength was at 463 nm. The absolute quantum yield (QY) of P-CDs in water solution was measured to be 4.1 %.

In order to study the stability of P-CDs, the impact of ionic strength on the stability of P-CDs was studied. The results (as shown in Fig. 3b) showed that when the concentration of sodium chloride changed within the limits of 0.0 ∼ 2.0 mol/L, the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs was basically unchanged, indicating that P-CDs had good salt tolerance. In addition, the consequences confirmed that the fluorescence strength of P-CDs abided above 90 % after excitation at 355 nm for 1 h (Fig. 3C), indicating that P-CDs had a certain anti-photobleaching ability. Moreover, the unique excitation dependence of P-CDs was also shown in Fig. 3d, which was in keeping with the possibility that energy traps and particle size distribution may be the cause of this phenomenon [34].

The cytotoxicity of P-CDs was an important means to evaluate its biocompatibility, and cell survival rate was an important criterion for evaluating cytotoxicity. Here, we combined CCK-8 method to investigate the cytotoxicity of P-CDs, and the different density ranges were 0, 12.5, 25, 50 and 100 mg/mL. As seen from Fig. 4 a, the survival rates of MC cells, HCT-116 cells and Hepa1-6 cells were 81.14 %, 91.36 % and 80.47 %, respectively. The cell survival rates of the three models were all above 80 %. Fig. 4 showed the fluorescent inverted microscope images of three model cells incubated, and electrodeposition was carried out with P-CDs of different concentrations. As was shown in Fig. 4b, MC cells in the control group were long spindle shaped, without striations, and they were at high concentration (100 µg/mL) and still remained intact after incubation (Fig. 4c). In Fig. 4d, HCT-116 cells in the control group grew up on the skin like adherent wall, and the morphology of HCT-116 cells was not changed significantly after 24 h incubation (Fig. 4e). Fig. 4f showed that Hepal-6 cells in the control group were polygonal, round and triangular, with large cell body and good morphology after high concentration P-CDs incubation for 24 h (Fig. 4g). The results showed that P-CDs prepared from waste pitaya seeds as carbon source had low cytotoxicity. It was further proved that P-CDs synthesized from pitaya seeds had good biocompatibility, which was suggested that the P-CDs developed in this work have potential uses in biomedical applications such as cell imaging, or as drug delivery systems and biosensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study the quenching mechanism of P-CDs by HA
ce:section.ce:para: In order to clarify the mechanism of HA quenching P-CDs, P-CDs and HA were tested by UV– Vis. From Fig. 3a, the shape of UV– Vis spectra of P-CDs with and without HA addition was basically unchanged, indicating that the dynamic quenching may affect the excited states of P-CDs. Fig. 5 a compared the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs with HA at different incubation temperatures. (25, 35, 45, 55, 65 and 75 °C) The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the system decreased with the increase of incubation temperature. It was speculated that with the increase of incubation temperature, the viscosity of the solution decreased and the bimolecular motion accelerated, thus increasing the diffusion trend and bimolecular quenching trend. All these indicated that there was a dynamic quenching process between HA and P-CDs.

As shown in Fig. 3a, there existed a partial convergence between the excitation spectra of P-CDs and the UV–vis spectra of HA, which indicated that the internal filtering effect (IFE) [35] and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) may be the mechanism of HA quenching P-CDs. The even fluorescence lifetime with and without HA addition was further studied (Fig. 5b). After the addition of HA, the fluorescence lifetime of P-CDs was reduced. It confirmed that there may exist FRET between HA and P-CDs. Under the excitation at the maximum excitation wavelength of 374 nm, the excitation of P-CDs as donor molecules can induce HA acceptor molecules to emit fluorescence, leading to attenuation of the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs, and then quenched.

So as to acquire high quality P-CDs, the synthesis conditions of P-CDs were optimized. The impacts of synthesis time on the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs were explored. Such as Fig. S6a, when the synthesis temperature was 180 °C and the synthesis time was 5 h, the fluorescence strength of P-CDs was the strongest. With the enlargement of synthesis temperature, the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs firstly enlarged and then decreased. Due to the augment of synthesis temperature or synthesis time, a growing number of carbon nuclei were produced and the fluorescence strength increased. With the further increase of synthesis temperature and time, the growth rate of carbon nuclei increased and gradually became larger, with the fluorescence intensity decreased. For the sake of studying the suitable pH range of P-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs affected by the pH value of the aqueous solution was studied. Magically, the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs remained relatively stable in hydrochloric acid-sodium hydroxide buffer solutions with the pH value away from 3.0 to 11.0. (Fig. S6b). At the same time, in order to further select the perfect conditions for the detection of P-CDs and HA, the suitable pH range of P-CDs and HA was studied. Fig. S6c demonstrated that the total fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution of P-CDs and HA had nearly no change from pH 3.0 to 11.0. Therefore, when the pH value was 7 (without acid and alkali), this method was more convenient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, reproducibility and stability of P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The coexistence of various ions in natural water usually quenched the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs. Similarly, the presence of HA structural analog FA in soil, a large amount of amino acid, and residual pesticides are also another major factor leading to the quenching of P-CDs. Therefore, in order to further apply to complex matrices, the consequence of various coexisting potential interfering substances about the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs was studied. It was manifest from Fig. 6 a that representative interfering substances (5 times of HA) and the mixture of these interfering substances were added to P-CDs solution（60 μg/mL). The deionized water was used as negative control. Compared with other potential interfering substances, the fluorescence intensity of HA was the lowest, indicating that P-CDs had good selectivity for HA (the yellow bar); The green bar showed that in the company of other potential interfering substances, the determination of HA with P-CDs was not affected by interfering components. These results pointed out that P-CDs had good selectivity and anti-interference capacity to HA under other related substances.

Stability was an important indicator for evaluating sensor performance. As shown in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs did not decay significantly under continuous measurement. In addition, the prepared P-CDs were stored in the refrigerator (4 °C) for 6 days and measured every day, and the fluorescence intensity value of P-CDs could still be maintained at 93 % of the initial value. However, after 6 days, the fluorescence intensity markedly decreased. It showed that the best use period of the prepared P-CDs is 6 days, which may be related to the biological matrix itself, such as pitaya seeds. Next, its reproducibility was evaluated. Under the same conditions, six tubes of P-CDs solution were prepared, and the RSD of the fluorescence intensity was 1.3 % when P-CDs were interacted with HA. The above results showed that P-CDs had a certain storage stability and reusability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of HA in water
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 a demonstrated that with the rise of HA concentration, the change of P-CDs fluorescence intensity. The results obtained that with the rise in concentration of HA, the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs decreased gently, and the half peak width of the fluorescence emission spectrum gradually widened by the complexation of carboxyl groups on the outside of P-CDs with HA. The illustration in Fig. 7b showed the acquaintance between the whole system of P-CDs with HA and the concentration of HA with the scope of 3.0–120.0 mg/L, which was well fitted with a good linear affiliation. The equation of linear regression was F/F0 = -0.0005 [HA] + 0.9 (R2 = 0.998). Fig. 7b showed the influence of diverse factors on P-CDs (60 µg/mL) fluorescence emission spectra in deionized water buffer solution (pH = 7.0) with or without different concentrations of HA (illustration: the linear relationship between HA concentration and F/F0 within the scope of 3.0 to 120.0 mg/L, where F was the fluorescence emission intensity of P-CDs with HA at 442 nm; F0 was the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs without HA at 422 nm). In order to evaluate the feasibility of this method in the actual sample detection, the actual samples of campus Lake 1 (longitude 123.701077, latitude 41.453126), campus Lake 2 (longitude 123.700562, latitude 41.453335), campus Lake 3 (longitude 123.701710, latitude 41.453070), tap water samples, soil of the campus flower bed (soil 1) and soil of the campus medicinal botanical garden (soil 2) were selected to detect HA. Due to the low content of HA in natural water, the standard addition method was used for detection. The HA content in the soil is relatively high. Thus, 15.0 g of the soil sample was weighed, 75 mL 0∙1mol/L Na4P2O7 and 0∙1mol/L NaOH solution were added in a boiling water bath, which could be then heated and extracted for 30 min. After that, cooled and centrifuged for 10 min (centrifuge speed: 5000 rpm) to obtain sample-treated clear liquid for subsequent determination. The recoveries and relative standard deviations of HA were listed in Table S1 and Table S2. It can be seen that the detection recovery rate of P-CDs for HA in water ranged from 96.4 % to 104.5 %, and the RSD% was less than 1.2. The detection of HA in soil samples was further realized. At the same time, compared this method with HPLC method, the results were not much difference, indicating that it could be a concise and promising approach for the detection of HA in real water and soil.

Compared with other related literatures [6,36–44], the results of HA detection were evaluated (Table S3).Similarly, not many reports were based on the detection of HA in water and soil, although it has been used for detection or as a platform for detecting other pollutants. In addition, the presence of lower concentrations of pollutants was problematic because these contaminants may escape during the removal process. Therefore, making eco-friendly sensors capable of monitoring low levels of pollutants in water was highly essential to ensure safety and better environmental sustainability. At present, there is no relevant report on the application of biomatrix-derived CDs for the detection of HA. In this work, pitaya seeds were selected as the precursor of P-CDs for the first time. The preparation process of P-CDs was simple, green, and environmentally friendly. Compared with the conventional methods, fluorescence analysis was used for detection, which avoided the tedious sample preparation process, and in most cases, adjusting the fluorescence signal enabled rapid detection, showing excellent response to HA. Furthermore, at room temperature conditions, the use of biocompatible materials to fabricate sensors was important to ensure cost reduction, environmental safety and minimal energy consumption. Therefore, P-CDs, which synthesized from pitaya seeds, provided a novel strategy for the detection of HA.

In this paper, a green, low-cost, preparation of pitaya seed fluorescent probe was developed for the quantitative detection of HA in natural water and soil. P-CDs had uniform particle size, good dispersion, high fluorescence intensity, and well excitation dependence. Besides, the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs was stable in the extent of ionic energy and pH value of sedimentary solution. CCK-8 method and microscopic observation showed that the P-CDs fixed by natural waste carbon source had low cytotoxicity. The method was simple, environment-friendly and reproducible, which can produce P-CDs with high quality and low toxicity. At the same time, a new method based on HA- induced fluorescence quenching of P-CDs was established to detect HA in water and soil. The results suggested that the method had good selectivity and high sensitivity. It made full use of the sensing platform of P-CDs, which can be used to detect HA in natural water and soil.

Chunling Mao: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Hanqing Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Heng Ye: Validation, Investigation. Longshan Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020-181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121950.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic degradation of humic acid in the presence of montmorillonite
Appl. Clay Sci.
